# TTS Script — Chrome DevTools MCP (SEO version)
**Blog:** docs.molecule.ai/blog/browser-automation-ai-agents-mcp
**Duration:** ~90s at ~150 wpm
**Date:** 2026-04-20
**Author:** Content Marketer (auto-generated)

---

Most AI agents hit the same wall: they can reason, plan, and call APIs — but the moment a task requires clicking through a website, filling a form, or reading a page that has no API, they're stuck.

The fix is giving your agent a real browser. Not a screenshot API, not a Playwright script written by a human. A browser your AI agent controls itself — deciding when to navigate, extract, and interact, the same way a human would.

The Model Context Protocol is the bridge. It gives AI models a standardized interface to call browser tools — not buried in a prompt, but as first-class, typed tool calls. Chrome DevTools Protocol is the engine: the same underlying protocol that powers Chrome DevTools, Puppeteer, and Playwright, exposed directly to your agent.

Before MCP, connecting an AI agent to a browser meant writing custom wrapper scripts or paying for a SaaS browser API. MCP solves both problems.

It gives you typed tool definitions — your agent sees browser navigate, dom query, page screenshot with JSON Schema inputs, not raw Python function names buried in a system prompt.

It gives you streaming tool calls — long-running browser operations stream progress back without blocking the agent's reasoning loop.

It gives you session persistence — CDP sessions maintain browser state across tool calls, so your agent isn't starting from a blank page every turn.

Molecule AI workspaces ship MCP browser tools as part of the standard runtime. If you're already on Molecule AI, browser automation is available — you configure which tools the agent can access, not how they work.

An MCP server that bridges to CDP maps Chrome DevTools Protocol domains onto MCP tool definitions. Your AI agent calls browser navigate and the MCP server translates it to a Page.navigate command over WebSocket.

The result: production-grade browser automation without Puppeteer wrappers, without SaaS dependencies, and without managing session state in your prompt.

For teams running browser automation in regulated environments, the governance layer matters as much as the automation itself. When an AI agent operates a browser on your behalf, it generates audit-relevant data — which sites it visited, what data it extracted, when sessions started and ended. Molecule AI's workspace isolation and org API key scoping give you that visibility by default.

Connect your own MCP server to a Molecule AI workspace using the built-in MCP tool registration. The platform handles the WebSocket lifecycle and tool call routing. You bring the browser logic.
